candidates.prompt: We're planning to shard our PostgreSQL users table by user_id using hash-based partitioning across 16 shards. We have about 50M rows today and expect 200M within 18 months. The main bottleneck right now is write throughput on the primary. We'll use Citus on top of Postgres. Grill me on this plan.
candidates.expected_output: A set of probing questions organized across multiple distinct branches: data distribution concerns, cross-shard query impact, operational readiness (migrations, backups, monitoring), alternatives to sharding, failure modes, and growth assumptions. No advice or endorsement — only questions. At least 8 questions total covering at least 4 branches.

assertions.name: covers-at-least-four-distinct-branches
assertions.criteria: The response addresses at least 4 clearly distinct topic areas (e.g., data distribution, cross-shard queries, operational concerns, alternatives, failure modes, growth assumptions). Count distinct thematic clusters of questions — there must be at least 4.

assertions.name: asks-about-operational-risk
assertions.criteria: At least one question explicitly addresses operational or risk concerns such as: how shards will be monitored, what happens when a shard goes down, how backups/restores work across shards, how schema migrations are coordinated, or how re-sharding would be performed if needed.
assertions.maps_to: Must-have: Asks about operational/risk concerns, not just design

assertions.name: questions-not-statements
assertions.criteria: At least 80% of the substantive sentences in the response are interrogative (end with '?' or are clearly framed as questions). The response does not contain paragraphs of advice, recommendations, or best-practice lectures.
assertions.maps_to: Neutrality: Asks questions, doesn't make statements or endorse/reject approaches

assertions.name: asks-at-least-eight-questions
assertions.criteria: The response contains at least 8 distinct questions (sentences ending in '?'). This tests relentlessness — the skill should not stop after 2-3 surface-level questions.
assertions.maps_to: Relentlessness: Keeps going — doesn't stop after 2-3 questions

assertions.name: questions-are-specific-to-plan
assertions.criteria: At least 3 questions reference specific details from the user's plan (e.g., hash-based partitioning, Citus, 50M/200M rows, user_id as shard key, 16 shards, write throughput bottleneck). Generic questions like 'have you considered alternatives?' without connecting to the stated plan do not count.
assertions.maps_to: Must-have: Questions are specific to the plan presented

candidates.discrimination_reasoning: Bare Claude tends to respond with advice ('you should consider X') and a short list of 2-3 concerns rather than a deep interrogation across many branches. It also tends to endorse Citus or suggest alternatives rather than asking about tradeoffs.

candidates.prompt: Our team of 6 backend engineers wants to break our Django monolith into microservices. We've identified 4 candidate services: auth, billing, notifications, and search. We plan to use gRPC between services and deploy on Kubernetes. The monolith currently handles about 2000 req/s. Grill me.
candidates.expected_output: Deep interrogation covering: motivation and what problem this actually solves, service boundary decisions, data ownership and cross-service transactions, deployment and rollback strategy, team topology alignment, operational complexity increase, and migration sequencing. All as questions, no prescriptive guidance.

assertions.name: probes-motivation-and-why
assertions.criteria: At least one question challenges or probes the underlying motivation — why microservices, what specific pain the monolith is causing, or whether the stated benefits have been validated. The skill should not take the decision to migrate as a given.
assertions.maps_to: Design insight: Questions reveal non-obvious concerns an expert would raise

assertions.name: covers-data-ownership-boundary
assertions.criteria: At least one question addresses data ownership, shared databases, cross-service transactions, or how data currently coupled in the monolith will be split across the 4 proposed services.
assertions.maps_to: Depth: Goes 2-3 levels deep on each topic

assertions.name: covers-team-or-org-concerns
assertions.criteria: At least one question addresses team structure, ownership, on-call responsibilities, or how 6 engineers will own and operate 4+ services. This is a non-obvious concern that a junior designer would miss.

assertions.name: no-endorsement-or-rejection
assertions.criteria: The response does not contain phrases that endorse the plan ('this is a good approach', 'microservices are the right choice') or reject it ('you should stick with the monolith', 'I'd recommend against'). It only asks questions.
assertions.maps_to: Deal-breaker: Endorsing or rejecting the plan without probing first

candidates.discrimination_reasoning: Bare Claude almost always offers opinions on monolith-to-microservices migrations ('make sure you have a good reason') and gives advice about service mesh, API gateways, etc. It rarely interrogates the team/org dimension or challenges the premise with sustained questioning.

candidates.prompt: I want to implement a feature flag system in-house instead of using LaunchDarkly or Split. The plan: store flags in a Redis hash, read them on each request with a thin SDK wrapper, and use our existing admin panel to toggle flags. We have about 30 engineers and 15 services. Grill me on this.
candidates.expected_output: Questions covering: build-vs-buy reasoning, operational maturity of the in-house solution, targeting/segmentation capabilities, audit trail and compliance, rollback and kill-switch scenarios, SDK distribution across 15 services, cache invalidation, and what happens when Redis is unavailable. Structured as pure interrogation.

assertions.name: probes-build-vs-buy-rationale
assertions.criteria: At least one question directly probes why building in-house is preferred over existing solutions — asking about cost, specific missing features, or what was evaluated and rejected. Not just mentioning it in passing.
assertions.maps_to: Design insight: Questions reveal non-obvious concerns an expert would raise

assertions.name: asks-about-failure-mode
assertions.criteria: At least one question asks about a failure scenario: what happens when Redis goes down, how stale flags are handled, whether there's a fallback, or how a bad flag rollout is detected and reversed.
assertions.maps_to: Must-have: Asks about operational/risk concerns, not just design

assertions.name: probes-missing-capabilities
assertions.criteria: At least one question probes capabilities the plan doesn't mention but a production flag system needs: user/segment targeting, percentage rollouts, audit logging, flag dependencies, or gradual rollout strategies.

assertions.name: asks-at-least-eight-questions
assertions.criteria: The response contains at least 8 distinct questions (sentences ending in '?'). The skill must be relentless and not stop after a few surface-level queries.

candidates.discrimination_reasoning: Bare Claude typically responds with a pros/cons list of build-vs-buy and recommends LaunchDarkly. It gives advice about what features to include rather than interrogating what's missing from the plan. It rarely asks 8+ questions.

candidates.prompt: We're migrating our CI/CD from Jenkins to GitHub Actions. Plan: rewrite all 40 Jenkinsfiles as GHA workflows over 3 months, run both in parallel during migration, then decommission Jenkins. We have a platform team of 3 who will do all the rewrites. Grill me.
candidates.expected_output: Questions covering: migration sequencing and prioritization, parallel-run cost and complexity, how parity is verified between old and new, what happens to custom Jenkins plugins, team capacity vs timeline, rollback if GHA doesn't work for certain pipelines, developer experience during transition, and success criteria.

assertions.name: questions-about-migration-sequencing
assertions.criteria: At least one question asks about how the 40 pipelines will be prioritized or sequenced for migration — not just 'will you migrate them all at once' but probing the ordering strategy, dependencies, or risk tiers.
assertions.maps_to: Depth: Goes 2-3 levels deep on each topic

assertions.name: questions-about-team-capacity
assertions.criteria: At least one question probes whether 3 platform engineers can realistically rewrite 40 pipelines in 3 months, or how their other responsibilities are handled during this period.

assertions.name: questions-about-verification-parity
assertions.criteria: At least one question asks how the team will verify that the new GHA workflows produce the same results as the Jenkins pipelines — how parity or correctness is validated during the parallel-run phase.
assertions.maps_to: Design insight: Questions reveal non-obvious concerns

assertions.name: no-advice-or-recommendations
assertions.criteria: The response does not contain imperative recommendations ('you should', 'make sure to', 'I recommend', 'consider using'). All guidance is framed as questions.
assertions.maps_to: Deal-breaker: Lecturing about best practices instead of interrogating

assertions.name: covers-at-least-four-distinct-branches
assertions.criteria: The response addresses at least 4 clearly distinct topic areas (e.g., migration strategy, team capacity, verification approach, rollback plan, developer experience, Jenkins-specific concerns, cost, timeline). Count distinct thematic clusters — there must be at least 4.

candidates.discrimination_reasoning: Bare Claude tends to give a checklist of migration best practices and recommend specific GHA features. It rarely questions whether the timeline is realistic for the team size, or how parity verification works. It also tends to give advice ('make sure you have a rollback plan') instead of asking questions about the rollback plan.
